finding: payload.unresolved_findings is defined as 'the count of the previous round's findings whose resolved.status is absent or not wontfix', but for the first round to record (round=1, recorded rounds=0) there is NO previous round. The value in that case is left undefined: a tester cannot tell whether unresolved_findings must be 0, the count of any pre-existing findings, or an error. §12.3 repeats 'count of non-wontfix previous findings' without covering the zero-prior-rounds case.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: State explicitly that when no round has been recorded yet (round=1) unresolved_findings is 0, and add a §12.3 assertion for the fresh-start case.

finding: §4.5 says tp resume 'produces ... the repo-relative path of the changed file' and §7.1 says paths are compared repo-relative '(the same base tp resume normalizes git status output to, §4.5)', but §4.5 never defines that normalization. `git status --porcelain` paths are relative to the invocation/working directory, so when tp resume runs from a subdirectory of the repo the paths (and thus keep-list matching and the `changes` array) will not be repo-relative and will silently mismatch keep-list entries. The referenced normalization is asserted but never specified.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Specify the mechanism: run git at the repository top-level (e.g. `git -C <toplevel> status --porcelain=v1 -z -uall`, deriving toplevel via `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`) so output is repo-root-relative, and state that both `changes`/`kept` paths and keep-list comparison use that repo-root base regardless of cwd.

finding: §5.2 and §12.8 say plain `tp config` reports the auto-detection result 'as an effective field (builtin or hc)', but do not pin WHERE that field lives in the JSON: a top-level `effective`, a `commit_strategy_effective` key, or a nested `commit_strategy.effective`. Two engineers would assert against different JSON paths, so the acceptance is not a deterministic test.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Pin the exact JSON shape, e.g. plain `tp config` emits `"commit_strategy": "auto"` plus `"commit_strategy_effective": "hc"` (or nested), and assert that exact key in §12.8.

finding: §5.2 states two rules that collide on the empty/absent case: 'The built-in default is auto' but also 'a commit_strategy value outside the three names ... resolves to builtin' with a stderr warning. The existing resolver (WorkflowOverride.CommitStrategy *string; resolved wf.CommitStrategy plain string whose built-in default is "") represents 'unset' as the empty string, which is itself 'outside the three names'. The spec never says whether truly-unset resolves to auto (the stated default) or to builtin (as an out-of-vocab value), nor how 'unset' is distinguished from a config that explicitly set an unknown/empty value, nor whether the warning fires for the default auto case. An implementer cannot decide the resolution of the most common (unconfigured) path.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Pin: when NO layer sets commit_strategy the effective strategy is `auto` with no warning; a PRESENT value not in {builtin,auto,hc} (including "" and legacy `squash`) resolves to `builtin` and warns. State how presence is detected (the *string pointers are nil at every layer) and that the built-in default constant changes from "" to "auto".

finding: §5.4 and §6 define single-task close under the effective `hc` strategy (`tp done --commit <sha>` repeatable, bare `tp done` rejected exit 2), but nowhere define `tp done --batch <ndjson>` under `hc`. Per CLAUDE.md the batch close is the *primary* close mechanism; the spec is silent on whether each NDJSON row must carry commit sha(s), whether a commit-less batch row is rejected exit 2 like the single path, and how per-row `commit_shas` are expressed. This is a P2 Batch Parity gap: what is now required for one task (a recorded commit) has no defined N-task equivalent.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Add a clause to §5.4 (and a §12.9 assertion) specifying batch close under `hc`: each `tp done --batch` row must carry a `commit`/`commit_shas` field (or `covered_by`), a bare row is rejected with the same exit-2 hc hint, and give the NDJSON row schema for multi-sha rows.

finding: §3.1 states categorically "either way the task carries `commit_shas` when closed" and frames the checkpoint invariant as "a task closed with its commit(s)". But §5.4.2 (and §12.9) explicitly allow closing a task with `tp done --covered-by <id>` "for a task that needs no commit" — such a task carries no `commit_shas`. The blanket §3.1 statement is false for covered-by closes, and the checkpoint-invariant wording does not accommodate the case where a unit's work is durably captured by *another* task's commit.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Qualify §3.1: a closed task carries `commit_shas` *unless it is closed `--covered-by` another task*, whose commit provides the save-point. State that a covered-by close satisfies the checkpoint invariant via the covering task's commit.

finding: §4.6 states blockers[].message is 'a human gloss of data already present in changes/payload' and that a machine consumer keys on code+class — the same human-gloss category as next_action.summary and kept[].reason. But §4.2's --compact omits only next_action.summary and each kept[].reason; blockers[].message is not on the strip list, so the token-stripping flag still ships per-blocker human prose the agent never parses.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Add blockers[].message to the --compact omission list (alongside next_action.summary and kept[].reason), leaving only the machine fields {code, class} per blocker under --compact.

finding: §4.4 (echoed in §12.3) defines `unresolved_findings` as "the count of the previous round's findings whose `resolved.status` is absent or **not `wontfix`**". This counts findings dispositioned `fixed` and `duplicate` as unresolved, contradicting tp's existing `tp review --resolve` model where `fixed`/`wontfix`/`duplicate` are all *terminal* resolutions. Singling out only `wontfix` over-reports remaining work (a fixed finding does not reappear; a duplicate is merged away), so the payload's headline count diverges from the resolve semantics an agent already relies on.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Define `unresolved_findings` as the count of previous-round findings with no terminal disposition, i.e. `resolved.status` absent (equivalently: not one of fixed/wontfix/duplicate). If counting fixed-but-unverified is intended, state that rationale and still exclude `duplicate`.

finding: §4.4.3 (and §12.3) say the implement payload surfaces 'the WIP task with wip: true', assuming a single wip task, but tp permits multiple wip tasks (`tp claim id1 id2`). Which wip task is surfaced when more than one exists is unpinned, so two engineers write contradictory assertions.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Pin the selection among multiple wip tasks (e.g. the one with the earliest started_at, or lowest id) and add a multi-wip assertion to §12.3.

finding: §4.5/§12.4 assert 'a rename reports the destination path', but git only emits an R (rename) porcelain status for a STAGED rename; an unstaged rename surfaces as a delete plus an untracked add (two separate paths). Whether the test stages the rename determines the observed output, so two engineers writing §12.4 get different results.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Specify that the destination-only rule applies to git-reported (staged) renames, and pin the unstaged-rename behavior (delete path + untracked destination both appear in changes).

finding: The `no-ready-task` blocker 'message includes the unmet dependency ids' (§4.6) / 'carries the unmet dependencies' (§4.4.3), but when several open tasks each have different unmet deps it is not pinned whether the ids are the union across all open tasks or those of a single chosen task. §12.3 ('naming the unmet deps') does not disambiguate.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: State that the blocker lists the union of unmet dependency ids across all open-not-ready tasks (or a single named task), and assert the exact set in §12.5.

finding: The `spec-stale` blocker fires only "while in `implement`/`audit`" (§4.6), and §4.3 scopes staleness surfacing to "a mid-implementation spec edit". But phase §4.3 rule 3 (all tasks done, audit converged) yields `release` regardless of the stale flag, and audit convergence is independent of the review stale flag (a spec edited after review converged leaves review stale yet audit can still read converged). So a spec edited after audit convergence reaches `release` with NO blocker — the exact drift (shipped code no longer matches spec) the stale mechanism exists to surface is dropped precisely at the release boundary.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Either emit `spec-stale` in `release` too (escalate) so the driver's terminal handoff flags the drift for the human, or state explicitly in §4.3/§4.6 why post-audit staleness is intentionally not surfaced.

finding: §4.6 blocker objects are exactly {code, message, class} and the section asserts 'The <count>/ids in a message are a human gloss of data already present in changes/payload; a machine consumer keys on code and class.' That holds for `unexplained-changes` (paths are in `changes`) but NOT for `no-ready-task`: §4.4.3 payload is {task:null, wip:false} with no dependency field, so the unmet-dependency ids exist ONLY inside the human `message`. A driver that wants to surface which deps are unmet must parse prose, contradicting the stated contract.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Either add a structured field to the no-ready-task payload/blocker (e.g. payload.blocked_by or a blocker `data`/`ids` field carrying the unmet dep ids) so the ids are machine-readable, or narrow the §4.6 'already present in changes/payload' claim to exclude no-ready-task.

finding: §4.6 claims a blocker message is only 'a human gloss of data already present in changes/payload', but for no-ready-task the unmet dependency ids live nowhere structured: §4.4.3's implement payload is {task: null, wip: false} with no dep list, and they are not in changes. A consumer that 'keys on code and class' must parse the human message to recover the blocking dep ids — the exact prose-scraping AX is meant to eliminate, contradicting the gloss claim.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Add the unmet dep ids as a structured field (e.g. implement payload gains blocked_by: [<id>], or the blocker carries a typed data), so the message becomes a true gloss; or narrow §4.6's 'already present in changes/payload' claim to exclude no-ready-task.

finding: §5.2/§12.8 add an `effective` field to plain `tp config` output but never pin its shape or presence: is it a top-level key, a sibling `commit_strategy_effective`, or nested under commit_strategy? Is it emitted only when strategy is `auto`, or always (effective==value for explicit builtin/hc)? Separately, §5.2 says `tp config --resolved` reports `value` = 'the configured name (builtin/auto/hc)', but an out-of-vocab configured value like `squash` is not one of those three — so it is unspecified whether `--resolved` shows the raw `squash` or the resolved `builtin`.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Specify exactly where `effective` sits in the plain `tp config` JSON and under which strategies it appears; and state whether `--resolved` `value` for an unknown configured string is the raw stored string or the resolved `builtin`.

finding: §5.4 enumerates behavior for `tp commit`, `tp done --auto-commit`, `tp done --commit`, `tp done --covered-by`, and bare `tp done`, but is silent on the low-level `tp close <id> <reason>` under effective `hc`. `tp close` records no commit, so under hc it would close a task with empty `commit_shas`, violating §3.1's 'either way the task carries commit_shas when closed'. An implementer cannot tell whether tp close is rejected (like bare tp done), requires pre-existing commit_shas, or is allowed to close uncommitted.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: State tp close's behavior under effective hc explicitly — e.g. reject a `tp close` that would leave commit_shas empty with exit 2 and the same hc hint, mirroring the bare-`tp done` rule.

finding: §6.1 asserts `commit_sha` is 'always set to commit_shas[0]', but §5.4 allows closing with `tp done --covered-by <id>` (no commit), leaving commit_shas empty. commit_shas[0] is then undefined, so the 'always' rule cannot hold and the resulting commit_sha value (null vs empty string) is unpinned for a no-commit close.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Add: when commit_shas is empty (covered-by / no-commit close) commit_sha is null, and assert this in §12.10.

finding: §7.2 defines `tp keep <path> "<reason>"` but never says how the <path> argument is normalized before storage. §7.1 requires entries be stored repo-relative, yet the command may be invoked from a subdirectory with a cwd-relative path, an absolute path, a `./`-prefixed path, or a trailing slash — and a glob argument (`*.log`) whose normalization is even less obvious. Without a stated rule the stored key may not match what §4.5 compares against.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: State that `tp keep` normalizes a non-glob <path> to a repo-relative path (rejecting or rebasing an absolute path outside the repo) before writing, and define how a glob argument is normalized (e.g. kept verbatim but interpreted relative to repo root) so keep-entries and git-status paths share one base.

finding: §7.3/§4.2 guarantee 'one entry per uncommitted file' in `kept`, but do not define which keep-list entry wins when a single file matches MULTIPLE entries (e.g. both `*.log` and an explicit `app.log`, or two overlapping globs). `kept[].reason` is 'the entry's reason' (singular) with no tie-break rule, so the surfaced reason — and the dedup-to-one guarantee — is left to implementer choice.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Pin the tie-break: the first matching keep-list entry in stored (insertion) order supplies the `reason`, and each file appears at most once in `kept`.

finding: §7.3/§4.5 copy 'the entry's reason' (singular) into kept, but when one uncommitted file matches MORE THAN ONE keep-list entry (e.g. both `*.log` and `app.log`) it is unpinned whether kept gets one entry (and which entry's reason wins) or a duplicate path per matching entry. §12.4 only tests one glob matching two files, not two entries matching one file.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Pin that a file matched by multiple entries yields exactly one kept entry using the first-matching (or last-written) entry's reason, and add that case to §12.4.

finding: L92 (marked fixed) flagged that a builtin project can close a task with no commit and leave its work uncommitted. The round-2 §3.1 rewrite adds recovery language ('an unexplained change'), but its summarizing clause 'Under builtin the commit is made by tp commit/--auto-commit; under hc it is made by the agent and recorded with tp done --commit; either way the task carries commit_shas when closed' still overclaims: §5.4.1 lists only the commit-making paths and does not forbid a bare builtin `tp done <id> "reason"`, which pre-0.28.0 ('all work as before') closes with empty commit_shas — a case §8.3 itself contemplates (warns on a successful close with uncommitted, non-kept changes). So 'either way ... carries commit_shas when closed' is not universally true.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Qualify the clause to the sanctioned commit-close, e.g. 'a sanctioned commit-close carries commit_shas; a builtin bare tp done (no commit) closes without commit_shas and its work surfaces as an unexplained change (§4.5, §8.3) to reconcile' — or explicitly state whether a bare builtin done is permitted.

finding: `changes` and `kept` are 'sorted lexicographically' (§4.2) but the collation is not pinned (byte-wise/ASCII vs case-insensitive vs Unicode). For paths mixing upper/lowercase (e.g. `README.md` vs `internal/`) two engineers could assert different orderings.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: State the sort is byte-wise (Go sort.Strings) over repo-relative paths, and include a mixed-case path in the §12.4 ordering assertion.

finding: §4.5 pins `-z` and 'for a rename, the destination path; the origin path is not separately reported', but does not state that a rename/copy entry consumes TWO NUL-separated tokens under `-z` (order reversed to `dest\0origin`). An implementer who does not consume-and-discard the second (origin) token will treat it as a phantom entry, wrongly adding it to `changes` and raising a spurious `unexplained-changes` blocker.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Add one sentence: under `-z` a rename entry is two NUL-separated tokens in `dest`-then-`origin` order; the parser reads the destination as the changed path and skips the following origin token (it is neither a `changes` nor a `kept` entry).

finding: The round-2 blocker rework added the claim: "The <count>/ids in a message are a human gloss of data already present in changes/payload; a machine consumer keys on code and class." This holds for unexplained-changes (count = len(changes), and changes is emitted §4.2 field 3) and spec-stale (spec path is §4.2 field 2), but is false for no-ready-task and the two budget-exhausted blockers: the unmet-dependency ids are carried ONLY in the message (§4.4 item 3 makes payload.task null with no deps field), and the cap value appears only in the message — neither is in changes or payload. A machine consumer that 'keys on code and class' and treats the message as glossy has no programmatic access to those ids/cap.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Narrow the claim to the blockers it is true for, e.g.: 'the count (unexplained-changes) mirrors changes and the path (spec-stale) mirrors the spec field; the no-ready-task ids and the budget cap appear only in the message and are recomputable from the task file / workflow config.' Or add a machine-readable slot (e.g. carry unmet-dep ids in payload when task is null).

finding: §5.2 keeps `tp config --resolved` uniform (`{value, source}`) by moving the auto-detect result into plain `tp config` as an "`effective` field (builtin or hc)". But plain `tp config` is documented (CLAUDE.md) as the flat "effective (resolved) config as JSON" — one scalar per setting. §5.2/§12.8 never specify the JSON placement of this new `effective` datum: nested object (`commit_strategy: {value, effective}`), sibling key (`commit_strategy_effective`), or present only when the value is `auto`. This makes plain `tp config`'s shape non-uniform for exactly one field with no defined structure, leaving a machine consumer unable to key on it deterministically (P4).

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Pin the plain-`tp config` JSON shape for the effective strategy (e.g. a top-level `commit_strategy_effective` key present only when `commit_strategy` resolves to `auto`), and add a §12.8 assertion on that exact shape.

finding: §6.1's BC justification for the commit_sha<->commit_shas mirror covers cross-version STORAGE (an old binary reads the stored commit_sha) but not agent-facing EMISSION. §6.2 makes single-commit the common case (builtin always records a single-element commit_shas), so a modern reader's tp show/report/list on such a task emits BOTH commit_sha:'a' and commit_shas:['a'] — the commit record doubled — and neither is on the --compact strip list.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Specify that modern agent-facing output emits the record once: omit the commit_sha mirror from non-legacy output (agents read commit_shas[0]), or have --compact drop commit_shas when it is a single element equal to commit_sha; keep the stored mirror purely for old-binary file compat.

finding: §7.2/§12.6 require `tp keep --list` to print 'the array as JSON' but do not pin the empty/absent case (given the project's documented nil-slice→null gotcha, [] vs null matters) nor the ordering of the printed array (insertion order after overwrites vs sorted), so the acceptance is not fully deterministic.

resolved.evidence: Round-3 revision: blockers gained machine-readable data field ({count}/{blocked_by}/{cap}/{spec}); pinned repo-root-relative path base for changes/kept/keep-list/tp-keep; tp config effective location (top-level commit_strategy_effective); absent-vs-present-unrecognized commit_strategy (absent->auto default, present-unknown->builtin); hc --batch per-row commit_shas/covered_by; covered-by close records no commit_sha; multi-match first-entry-wins; unresolved_findings=0 for round 1; byte-wise sort; staged-vs-unstaged rename; tp keep --list empty->[]; compact strips blocker message.

suggestion: Pin that `tp keep --list` prints `[]` (never null) when empty and defines a fixed order (insertion or sorted by path); assert the empty case in §12.6.
